coredata.dc:title: Dual integration of amine-functionalized carbon dots endowed nanofiltration membranes with highly efficient biofouling/ acid/chlorine resistance for effective Mg2+/Li+ separation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuan
Zou, Wen-Sheng
Kong, Weili
Chen, Xia
Li, Weihua
Huang, Xianhuai
Wang, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: Reports on nanofiltration (NF) membranes with acid/chlorine/biofouling resistance remain limited, resulting in a significant challenge. In this work, amine-functionalized carbon dots (Am-CDs) and nanocomposites (Am-CDs/PL) were fabricated using citric acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors and coupling with Am-CDs on polylysine (PL) via catalysis. The Am-CDs were first integrated into the polyamide (PA) layer of a polyethersulfone substrate through interfacial polymerization (IP) (denoted as I-Am-CDs-M), and then the Am-CDs/PL was continuously grafted on the PA layer through secondary IP (denoted as II-Am-CDs-M). The pristine membrane was synchronously prepared by IP between trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and piperazine (PIP) for contrast (denoted as Contrast-M). The Am-CDs exhibited outstanding oxidase activity and acid/ClO− response performance. The dual integration of Am-CDs significantly improved the wettability and positive charge of the modified NF membranes, endowing them with excellent separation performance. Notably, I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M naturally inherited the oxidase activity of the Am-CDs, contributing to eminent anti-bacterial functionality. Furthermore, Am-CDs vested these membranes with good anti-acid and anti-chlorine performance through the generation of reversible C–NH3 + forms and the oxidization of some amines as nitryl groups and chlorination, respectively, which helped decrease the maintenance costs and prolonged the operational lifespan. II-Am-CDs-M was successfully applied for Mg2+/Li+ separation. This strategy provided a novel protocol for designing and fabricating NF membranes with highly efficient separation performance.

head.ce:title: Dual integration of amine-functionalized carbon dots endowed nanofiltration membranes with highly efficient biofouling/ acid/chlorine resistance for effective Mg2+/Li+ separation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interfacial Process and Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, Anhui Jianzhu University, Anhui, Hefei, 230026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interfacial Process and Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Resource Reuse

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interfacial Process and Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, Anhui Jianzhu University, Anhui, Hefei, 230026, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reports on nanofiltration (NF) membranes with acid/chlorine/biofouling resistance remain limited, resulting in a significant challenge. In this work, amine-functionalized carbon dots (Am-CDs) and nanocomposites (Am-CDs/PL) were fabricated using citric acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors and coupling with Am-CDs on polylysine (PL) via catalysis. The Am-CDs were first integrated into the polyamide (PA) layer of a polyethersulfone substrate through interfacial polymerization (IP) (denoted as I-Am-CDs-M), and then the Am-CDs/PL was continuously grafted on the PA layer through secondary IP (denoted as II-Am-CDs-M). The pristine membrane was synchronously prepared by IP between trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and piperazine (PIP) for contrast (denoted as Contrast-M). The Am-CDs exhibited outstanding oxidase activity and acid/ClO− response performance. The dual integration of Am-CDs significantly improved the wettability and positive charge of the modified NF membranes, endowing them with excellent separation performance. Notably, I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M naturally inherited the oxidase activity of the Am-CDs, contributing to eminent anti-bacterial functionality. Furthermore, Am-CDs vested these membranes with good anti-acid and anti-chlorine performance through the generation of reversible C–NH3 + forms and the oxidization of some amines as nitryl groups and chlorination, respectively, which helped decrease the maintenance costs and prolonged the operational lifespan. II-Am-CDs-M was successfully applied for Mg2+/Li+ separation. This strategy provided a novel protocol for designing and fabricating NF membranes with highly efficient separation performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium, an important strategic metal, exhibits outstanding high redox potential and is widely used in the field of electrochemistry, as it can efficiently decrease fossil energy consumption and greatly contribute to carbon peak and carbon neutralization at a scheduled time [1–5]. Lithium battery plants consume the largest amount of lithium resources, which are involved in two main aspects. Specifically, the development of electric vehicles as alternatives to fuel vehicles has led to a growing number of lithium requirements [6–8]. Additionally, portable rechargeable devices, especially mobile phones, have further increased lithium demand. These factors have resulted in a scarcity in annual lithium supply. Statistics have shown that home appliances and other equipment using lithium batteries as a power supply have experienced a nearly 3-fold increase in use from 2010 to 2020, with the annual growth rate of global lithium consumption exceeding 30% [2,9]. By 2025, global lithium consumption is predicted to reach striking 95,000 tons [3]. Because the conventional ore extraction of solid lithium results in high energy consumption and environmental damage [10], current research has focused on recovery from liquid lithium resources [11,12]. As summarized by the U.S. Geological Survey, global liquid lithium resources, such as salt-lake/geothermal brine and seawater, account for nearly 60% of the total lithium reserves [13], which are dispersed mainly in South America, North America, East Asia, and Australia [14,15]. More than 80 % of the total lithium reserves in salt-lake brine are found in China, with gross reserves reaching 4,500,000 tons [16–18]. Accordingly, considering the large aggregate demand and surging demand growth, effective technology for the separation and purification of lithium in salt-lake brine and seawater has become essential.

Metal ions typically coexist in lithium-rich brine and seawater, making high-purity lithium recovery significantly more difficult [19]. This is especially true for Mg2+, which contains a hydrated ionic radius and chemical features similar to those of lithium ions (Li+), with the extraction efficiency and the product purity of Li + are strongly influenced by the high abundance of Mg2+ in brine [11]. Therefore, the development of actual technologies to determine the concentration and purification of lithium remains extremely complicated and challenging. Advanced technologies such as chemical precipitation [20,21], physical/chemical adsorption [22,23], solvent extraction [24], electrodialysis [25–28] and membrane separation [29–35] have been developed to extract lithium from salt-lake brine. Among these, membrane separation technology is highly favored and serves as one of the most promising lithium recovery strategies due to its low energy consumption, simple operation, and environmental friendliness [36]. Separation membranes can selectively sieve Li+ from Mg2+ abundant brine without chemical reagents or organic solvents, effectively achieving the precise separation of Li+ by a charged porous surface [37–39]. Polyamide (PA) nanofiltration (NF) membranes exhibit a low rejection efficiency for monovalent ions but a high rejection efficiency for multivalent ions based on the Donnan effect [40–43]. The PA NF membranes can efficiently separate monovalent ions from mixed mono-/divalent ions under the synergistic effects of size sieving and the Donnan effect [44,45]. However, PA NF membranes have issues with biofouling and poor acid-/chlorine tolerance, which primarily originate from the PA active layer. Acidolysis can occur at the amide bond site of the active layer, resulting in the decomposition and disappearance of the selective permeability of PA [46]. Due to the high hydrophobicity and inferior smoothness of the skin layer, bacteria will have a tendency to irreversibly adhere to and grow on the surface [47]. Once biofilm formation occurs, physical and chemical cleaning, membrane modification, and backwashing may mitigate but completely eliminate membrane fouling, resulting in the enhanced water flux hindrance [48,49]. In addition, with the long-term exposure of the NF membrane to reactive chlorine species, an irreversible aromatic substitution reaction or reversible N-chlorination reaction will occur, distorting the amide bonds, and subsequently, causing degradation of the polymer chain [50–52]. As a result, thin-film composite (TFC) PA NF membranes are urgently needed for effective Mg2+/Li+ separation, featuring synchronous and highly efficient acid/biofouling/chlorine resistance to reduce maintenance costs and extend the membrane lifespan during membrane operation.

The decoration of carbon dots (CDs) nanozymes with anti-acid/-chlorine characteristics on NF membranes serves as a promising solution. In this study, amine-functionalized CDs (Am-CDs) were synthesized using polyethylenimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) as carbon resources and subsequently characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), zeta-potential analysis, Fourier transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The nanocomposites (Am-CDs/PL) coupled with Am-CDs on polylysine (PL) were fabricated via 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydro/N-hydroxysuccinimide (EDC/NHS) catalysis, and the optical properties of the Am-CDs, including the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths, excitation-dependent emission behaviors, and lifetime, were studied. Then, the enzymatic activity, pH, and ClO−-dependent emissions of the Am-CDs were determined. The Am-CDs were first coupled into the PA layer of the PES substrate through an interfacial polymerization (IP) (denoted as I-Am-CDs-M), and then Am-CDs/PL were continuously grafted onto the PA layer through secondary IP (SIP) (denoted as II-Am-CDs-M). I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M, along with the pristine membranes obtained by IP between TMC and PIP (denoted as Contrast-M), were characterized by analysis of their attenuated total reflectance FTIR (ART-FTIR), contact angle analysis, and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The NF membranes were used for Mg2+/Li+ separation, and their separation performance was comprehensively evaluated. The acid-/biofouling-/chlorine-resistant features of Contrast-M, I-Am-CDs-M, and II-Am-CDs-M were subsequently investigated and their anti-acid/-chlorine/-biofouling mechanisms were elucidated in detail.

The CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal approach according to our previous report with slight modifications [53]. Specifically, 0.3 g of PEI was first dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water under rapid stirring for 30 min. After a transparent mixture formed, 1.0 g of CA was gradually added to the solution and continuously stirred for another hour. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a Teflon®-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The naturally cooled orange suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate any large particles. The supernatant was further purified in a dialysis bag for 2 days to clean the unreacted small molecules, and a gray powder was obtained by freeze-drying and then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The CDs were denoted as Am-CDs, as amine groups covered the surface (vide post).

EDC (4.0 mM) was added to a solution containing 50 μg/mL of PL (20 mL) at a pH of 6.0. The mixture was then slowly stirred for 10 min, after which the same molar concentration of NHS was added. After stirring for another 30 min, 50 mg of Am-CDs was gradually poured into the above solution. The resulting mixture was allowed to stand overnight and subsequently purified in a dialysis bag for 3 days to clean the small molecules and a portion of the unreacted Am-CDs. A white powder was obtained from freeze-drying and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use. The nanocomposites synthesized by integrating Am-CDs on PL were denoted as Am-CDs/PL.

NF membranes were fabricated through the co-IP of the Am-CDs and PIP with TMC, and the subsequent secondary integration of Am-CDs/PL onto the PA layer by SIP. The tailored rectangular PES substrate (5 cm × 8 cm) was completely soaked in a solution containing a certain quantity of Am-CDs (x wt%, x = 0, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5) and corresponding PIP content ((1.0 – x) wt%) for 2 min. This was followed by the removal of excess aqueous solution on the surface using compressed air. Subsequently, 0.15 wt% TMC/hexane solution was poured onto the membrane surface to activate the IP reaction for 2.0 min, during which the thin and dense PA layer containing Am-CDs covered the surface of the PES substrate membrane. The unreacted TMC/hexane solution was cleaned from the membrane surface by compressed air prior for 15 min and dried at 60 °C to further eliminate excess hexane and simultaneously enhance the density of the PA layer. The as-prepared membranes were denoted as (0.2–0.5)-I-Am-CDs-M, and the membrane without Am-CDs in the PA layer was subsequently denoted as Contrast-M. A portion of I-Am-CDs-M was dried in an oven for SIP, while the remainder was deposited in double deionized water (DDW) for other uses.

SIP was conducted to further couple Am-CDs/PL onto I-Am-CDs-M. I-Am-CDs-M with an outward PA layer was smoothly sandwiched between the rectangular glass backing plate and rectangular frame with a thickness of 0.25 cm using four clamps. The Am-CDs/PL aqueous solution was poured into the frame and reacted between the residual acyl chloride and amine of Am-CDs/PL for 3 min. Subsequently, the outward membrane surface was washed with DDW several times to clean the uncoupled CDs/PL. The as-obtained membrane was denoted as II-Am-CDs-M and maintained in DDW.

Two pieces of identical NF membranes were selected. One of the NF membranes was immersed in an aqueous solution containing 0.1 mg/mL sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) at a pH of 5.0 for different durations. The membrane was then removed and washed with a large amount of DDW to eliminate residual NaClO. This process was conducted in the dark and denoted as chlorination treatment. An acid resistance test was conducted using the same approach as above at room temperature, where the only change was the pH of HCl, which was 3.0 instead of the 0.1 mg/mL NaClO solution. Similarly, this process was referred to as acidization treatment. The changes in salt rejection and permeate flux of the untreated and treated membranes for different durations were used to characterize the chlorine/acid resistant properties at an operating pressure of 3 bar.

As an emerging class of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials, CDs have attracted increasing attention due to the following significant advantages. 1) CDs exhibit excellent photophysicochemical properties, mainly including good water solubility, high biocompatibility, outstanding photostability, easy derivation, and tunable emission. In particular, the cytotoxicity of CDs was found to be far lower than that of traditional organic dyes and semiconducting quantum dots, exhibiting a powerful potential as next-generation contrast agent. 2) CDs are inexpensive and easily available precursors. Chemicals with high purity as carbon sources are not highly desired, with almost all biomass materials reported for synthesizing CDs. 3) Simple synthesis approaches and purification processes are also characteristics of CDs, with temperatures lower than 200 °C generally used for carbonization and simple dialysis for purification. 4) Moreover, CDs have been identified as artificial enzyme mimics, with their enzymatic activities not affected by temperature or pH. Given the abovementioned advantages, the integration of CDs onto PA membranes by IP and SIP endows PA membranes with good hydrophilicity, low roughness, and antibacterial properties, decreasing the maintenance costs, extending the service life, and improving the work efficiency [54,55]. To achieve better membrane separation performance, we first fabricated and characterized Am-CDs using PEI and CA as the precursors by one-pot hydrothermal approach (Scheme 1 A). The XRD pattern shows that the Am-CDs had an amorphous structure, and the TEM image indicates that the Am-CDs were uniformly dispersed. Diameter values ranging from 2 to 6 nm, and a center at 3.8 nm, were observed. The zeta-potential of 7.08 mV clearly indicates that the Am-CDs were positively charged, and both the XPS and FTIR spectra reveals that many more amine groups and fewer carboxyl groups covered the surface of Am-CDs, which coincided with the zeta-potential results (1.2. Apparatus and Characterizations, and 2. Preparation and characterization of Am-CDs are presented in the Supplementary Information (SI)).

The photophysicochemical performances of the Am-CDs are the foundation for their use as nanozymes. Therefore, the UV–vis absorption, maximum excitation and emission spectra, as well as excitation-dependent emission behaviors were surveyed. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime were assessed. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, two adsorption peaks at 245 and 330 nm were observed in the normalized UV–vis spectrum, which were rooted in the π-π* transition of the carbon cores and the n-π* transition of the nitrogen of the amine and the oxygen of the carbonyl groups. A broad shoulder absorption greater than 330 nm could be attributed to the N and O-containing functional groups around the edges of the Am-CDs [56]. The normalized maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the Am-CDs at 370 and 450 nm, respectively, were measured. The emissions could be characterized as defect-state decay due to the inconsistency of the main excitation bands with the corresponding excitonic absorption bands [57]. Under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, the Am-CDs aqueous solution emitted bright blue fluorescence, signifying the high QY of the Am-CDs (inset in Fig. 1A). The mean lifetime was triexponentially fitted as 4.2 ns (Table S1), suggesting that the three decay channels responded to the blue fluorescence of the Am-CDs (Fig. 1B). The effect of excitation wavelength on emission was also considered. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 410 nm, a corresponding redshift in emission wavelength occurred (inset in Fig. 1C), and the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 370 and 450 nm were observed, respectively (Fig. 1C). Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY of Am-CDs was evaluated as 36.7%, resulting in a rather high QY, which corresponded to the bright blue fluorescence, implying high enzymatic activity of the Am-CDs (Fig. 1D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-bacterial activities and pH/ClO−-dependent emissions of the Am-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the excellent photophysicochemical performances of the Am-CDs, the anti-bacterial activity and other potential applications of the Am-CDs were studied, and the anti-bacterial activity of the strains was preferentially explored (1.5. Anti-bacterial property tests are provided in the SI). Escherichia coli (E. coli) was co-cultured as a model strain with 10 mg/L of Am-CDs under light irradiation. Synchronously, a control sample was also fabricated under the same conditions, but without the addition of Am-CDs. The samples were then plated for colony-counting after 1 h of illumination. The bacterial colonies were barely visible on the plate with the Am-CDs but filled the entire plate without the Am-CDs. Significantly more bacterial colonies were present without Am-CDs than with Am-CDs (Fig. 2 A and B). According to the quantitative analysis, the concentration of E. coli was 1.07 × 104 CFU/mL in the sample containing Am-CDs. The bacterial inhibition rate was calculated as 96.2% for the sample with Am-CDs, and it infinitely approached 0% for the sample without Am-CDs, clearly demonstrating that the Am-CDs exhibited significant biofouling resistance.

In addition, the pH/ClO−-dependent emission behaviors and UV–vis absorption spectra of the Am-CDs were considered. As shown in Fig. 2C, when the pH of the Am-CDs aqueous solution was adjusted, a change in fluorescence intensity and redshift in emission were observed. When the pH was increased from 3.8 to 11.2, the emission intensity gradually increased and subsequently decreased via a stable platform with a pH from 6.2 to 9.6 (inset in Fig. 2C). In addition, the corresponding pH-dependent UV–vis absorption data were collected. Surprisingly, a broad absorption platform similar to fluorescence was observed at an approximate pH range (Fig. 2D). The effects of adding a small amount of HCl or dilution on the pH of the Am-CDs aqueous solution were investigated using NaOH aqueous solution as the control. The change in pH of the Am-CD aqueous solution was significantly lower than that in the NaOH aqueous solution (Fig. S6), demonstrating that the Am-CDs could efficiently resist the influence of acid addition or dilution to maintain the pH of the system. The XPS spectra provided identical results. Due to the amine and carboxyl groups covering the surfaces of the Am-CDs, these Am-CDs could be utilized as electrolytes similar to amino acids to buffer the effects of external stimuli.

Under the same experimental conditions, but using NaClO instead of HCl, the effects of NaClO on the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption were studied. When NaClO was dosed in the Am-CDs aqueous solution, the fluorescence intensity continuously decayed (Fig. 2E), and fluorescence signals ranging from bright blue to the final light were observed (inset in Fig. 2E). The UV–vis absorption curves before and after treatment with NaClO show distinct differences. The absorption peaks at 245 and 329 nm and weak shoulder absorption ranging from 400 to 600 nm in the curve before treatment, as well as at 331, 380, 495, and 580 nm in the curve after treatment were observed (Fig. 2F). In particular, the absorption at 330 nm attributed to the n-π* transition in the treated curve indicates that the amine groups were not oxidized by NaClO. These changes in fluorescence and UV–vis absorption with the addition of HCl and NaClO suggested that both the protons and NaClO could interact with the Am-CDs to form new species, and the interaction sites were possibly located at the amine groups [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of nanofiltration membranes
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted above, the Am-CDs had an eminent antibacterial capacity, and potential anti-acid/-chlorine abilities. We expected to conjugate the Am-CDs into/onto the PA NF membrane, endowing the membrane with the same biofouling/acid/chlorine resistance performances as the Am-CDs. Accordingly, in addition to the Am-CDs, nanocomposites Am-CDs/PL were fabricated by coupling the Am-CDs to PL under the catalysis of EDC/NHS (Scheme 1B). We obtained I-Am-CDs-M through IP by mixing PIP with different amounts of Am-CDs and TMC (Scheme 1C). Then, II-Am-CDs-M with different amounts of Am-CDs were prepared through the integration of Am-CDs/PL on the surface of the corresponding I-Am-CDs-M by SIP (Scheme 1D). For comparison, Contrast-M was also obtained by IP between PIP and TMC, and the effects of Am-CDs amount on the water flux (Jw) of the I− and II-Am-CDs-Ms were tested to select the optimized membranes. Both I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M with 0.4 wt% of the Am-CD content showed maximum Jw value (Fig. S7). As a result, these I- and II-Am-CDs-Ms were applied in subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence spectra, ATR-FTIR spectra, water contact angles, AFM results, and SEM images were performed to characterize these membranes. As shown in Fig. 3 , Contrast-M could emit fluorescence at 412 nm due to the aromatic ring of TMC. Significantly stronger fluorescence at 408 nm for I-Am-CDs-M and the strongest fluorescence at 402 nm for II-Am-CDs-M were observed due to the first and second integration of the Am-CDs, respectively (Fig. 3A). These results indicate that the Am-CDs and Am-CDs/PL were successfully twice conjugated onto/into the NF membranes. The ATR-FTIR spectra reveal that the peaks at 1577 and 1485 cm−1 could be assigned to the C–N stretching and N–H bending vibrations of the amides, respectively (Fig. 3B) [54,55]. The continuous increase in signals of the two groups from Contrast-M to I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M also confirmed that the Am-CDs and Am-CDs/PL were successfully coupled into the PA layer and onto the PA layer, respectively.

The influence of dual integration of Am-CDs on the hydrophilicity and roughness of the I- and II-Am-CDs-M was mainly emphasized. The water contact angles of the three pieces of membranes were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the contact angles of 52.3° for Contrast-M, 34.1° for I-Am-CDs-M, and 31.6° for II-Am-CDs-M were achieved. Overall, Contrast-M demonstrated the worst hydrophilicity. Due to the favorable hydrophilicity of the Am-CDs, the first conjugation of the Am-CDs for the PA layer (IP) significantly improved the surface hydrophilicity of the I-Am-CDs-M, and the second coupling of the Am-CDs/PL onto the surface of the I-Am-CDs-M (SIP) further improved wettability. The decreased contact angles, along with the XPS spectra, reveal a considerable number of hydrophilic groups, including amine and carboxyl groups covered on the membrane surfaces. In addition, the surface smoothness of the membranes was determined. Ra values of 153 nm for Contrast-M, 210 nm for I-Am-CDs-M, and 176 nm for II-Am-CDs-M were obtained (Fig. 3D). Other than the improvement in surface hydrophilicity after integrating the Am-CDs and Am-CDs/PL, the conjugation of Am-CDs onto Contrast-M contributed to an increase in surface roughness, which indicated that the Am-CDs were conformally and chemically bonded to the membrane surface, resulting in the deterioration of the surface topography. For SIP, as well as conformal conjugation, Am-CDs/PL preferentially filled the surface ravines, contributing to better smoothness of II-Am-CDs-M than that of I-Am-CDs-M but still inferior to that of Contrast-M. The SEM images at different magnifications were obtained to further characterize the NF membranes. As shown in Fig. 3E and F, Contrast-M containing numerous ravines underwent dual integration of the Am-CDs and Am-CDs/PL, and the diameter of the ravines decreased; however, the drop heights between the top and valley bottoms were both greater than those of Contrast-M. These results are in good agreement with the surface roughness measurements. Overall, the decoration of Am-CDs and Am-CDs/PL led to deteriorative smoothness, but it comprehensively strengthened the surface wettability of the NF membranes [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance and operational stability of nanofiltration membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the separation performance of the three as-prepared NF membranes, experiments with 1000 ppm concentrations of different inorganic salts, such as MgSO4, Na2SO4, MgCl2, and LiCl, as well as feed solutions and operating pressure at 3 bar were performed. The results in Fig. 4 A show that regardless of the type of inorganic salt used as a feed solution, the water permeances sharply increased after initial integration of the Am-CDs, which slightly increased after secondary conjugation of Am-CDs/PL. Compared to Contrast-M, mean growth multiples in a water flux of 2.118 for I-Am-CDs-M and 2.306 for II-Am-CDs-M were obtained, resulting in the improvement of water permeance after decoration of the Am-CDs. Moreover, except for LiCl, which consisted of a monovalent anion and cation salt, the three NF membranes exhibited higher and gradually improved salt rejection when the three other inorganic salts were used as feed solutions (Fig. 4B). Except for LiCl, mean salt rejections of 83.03% for Contrast-M, 91.27% for I-Am-CDs-M, and 96.67% for II-Am-CDs-M were obtained. Similarly, In comparison with Contrast-M, the mean growth multiples in salt rejection, 1.099 for I-Am-CDs-M and 1.164 for II-Am-CDs-M, were calculated. The growth multiples for salt rejection seemed to be very low but, in reality, were not low due to the relatively high salt rejection of Contrast-M. Furthermore, the PEG rejection results show that the corresponding MWCO values are 472 Da for II-Am-CDs-M, 530 Da for I-Am-CDs-M, and 761 Da for Contrast-M. The pore sizes can be estimated to be 1 nm for II-Am-CDs-M, 1.1 nm for I-Am-CDs-M, and 1.3 nm for Contrast-M using the Stokes-Einstein formula (Fig. S11). The MWCO values further confirm that the electro-static repulsion (Donnan effect) has an important contribution to the salt rejection capability of the membranes. These results indicate that the modification of Am-CDs could greatly promote the separation performance of the NF membranes, and the improvement in water flux and salt rejection can be attributed to the high hydrophilicity and highly positive charge of Am-CDs.

The operational stability of the NF membranes played a key role in their practical application. The effects of operation time and pressure on the water permeance and salt rejection were assessed using MgCl2 as the feed solution. After long-term continuous operation for 80 h, the water flux and salt rejection of the NF membranes were relatively smooth, despite some fluctuations (Fig. 4C and D). Mean water fluxes of 4.12 for Contrast-M, 8.76 for I-Am-CDs-M, and 9.74 for II-Am-CDs-M (L·m−2 h−1) were achieved. In addition, the corresponding salt rejections for Contrast-M, I-Am-CDs-M, and II-Am-CDs-M were 82.96%, 93.45%, and 97.02%, respectively, which were consistent with the previous results (Fig. 4A and B). These results suggest that the NF membranes were rather stable in terms of water permeance and salt rejection. The water permeabilities at different operation pressures were considered in this work. As shown in Fig. 4E, with increasing operation pressure from 1 to 5 bar, the water fluxes increased. According to the slope of water flux versus operating pressure, the growth sequences were II-Am-CDs-M, I-Am-CDs-M, and Contrast-M in descending order. However, three water fluxes per bar of operating pressure, 1.54 for Contrast-M, 3.49 for I-Am-CDs-M, and 3.80 for II-Am-CDs-M (L·m−2 h−1 bar−1), remained almost unchanged (Fig. S8). The salt rejection of the NF membranes was slightly augmented with increasing operating pressure (Fig. 4F). Therefore, dual integration of the Am-CDs endowed the membranes with outstanding separation performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofouling/acid/chlorine resistant performances of NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the biofouling resistance of the Am-CDs and the acid/chlorine response to the Am-CDs, the anti-bacterial/-acid/-chlorine potentials of the three NF membranes were assessed using E. coli as the model bacterium (Scheme 1E). The anti-bacterial properties of the membranes were determined using the same method as the one used for the Am-CDs (1.5. Anti-bacterial property tests, SI). The samples of the different NF membranes were divided into two groups, namely, the dark group and the light irradiation group, for comparison. As shown in Fig. 5 , the plates in the dark group demonstrated very poor anti-bacterial activity, and E. coli was observed over the entire agar plates. The number of bacterial colonies that grew on II-Am-CDs-M, I-Am-CDs-M, and Contrast-M increased in ascending order, which could be attributed to the positive charges of the Am-CDs. Conversely, the corresponding light irradiation group exhibited markedly fewer bacterial colonies than the dark group. The number of bacterial colonies, in descending order, was as follows: Contrast-M, I-Am-CDs-M, and II-Am-CDs-M. Only a few colonies were found, especially for I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M, as a result of the nanozymetic activity of the Am-CDs (see below). The above results verify that conjugation of the Am-CDs endowed I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M with excellent biofouling resistance.

The effects of chlorination and acidification time on the water flux and salt rejection were evaluated to characterize the anti-acid/-chlorine performances of Contrast-M, I-Am-CDs-M, and II-Am-CDs-M. Prior to evaluation, the chlorination/acidization time-dependent water contact angles of the three pieces of NF membranes were tested. For every piece of membrane, an opposite trend, i.e., a continuous increase with increased chlorination time but a persistent decrease with increased acidification time, was observed for the water contact angles, which were rooted in the nitration (C–NO2) and protonation (C–NH3 +) of the amine groups (vide post). For every interval of measurement time, the same descending order as that of the zero hour (untreated) membranes was observed (Fig. 6 A and D). The gradual decrease in membrane wettability with longer chlorination time contributed to the continuous decrease in water flux and salt rejection of the three NF membranes (Fig. 6B and C). Specifically, after chlorination for 8 h, a sudden increase in water flux and a sharp decrease in salt rejection were observed due to possible damage to the amide bonds of Contrast-M caused by chlorination. The persistent improvement in membrane wettability with longer acidification time resulted in a slight increase in both water flux and salt rejection of the I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M (Fig. 6E and F). Similar to the reason for chlorination, the possible acidolysis of the PA layer caused by the acidization led to a constant increase in water flux and a subsequent decrease in salt rejection in Contrast-M. Notably, the abovementioned results indicate that I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M exhibited outstanding anti-bacterial/-acid/-chlorine effects, with the much better performance of the latter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of biofouling/acid/chlorine resistance of the NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Exploring the mechanisms of biofouling/acid/chlorine resistance of the NF membranes was conducive to designing and fabricating novel membrane materials for extending the operating lifespan and decreasing maintenance costs. Under light irradiation, the EPR spectrum of the Am-CD aqueous solution containing DMPO was obtained. As shown in Fig. 7 A, a quadruple peak with a 1:1:1:1 intensity ratio was observed in the EPR curve, which was the characteristic peak of the superoxide anion radical (O2 −·) [59]. These results indicate that the Am-CDs exhibited oxidase activity that could generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) capable of sterilization under light irradiation. In addition, a colorimetric assay based on TMB was performed to verify O2 −· as an anti-bacterial agent [60]. After light irradiation for 1 min, the colorless TMB aqueous solution and blue the mixture of the Am-CDs and TMB were observed. However, when superoxide dismutase (SOD), an O2 −· scavenger, was added to the mixed solution, the color remained unchanged, further confirming the oxidase activity of the Am-CDs (Fig. 7B) [61]. Notably, I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M naturally inherited oxidase activity due to the dual integration of Am-CDs, suggesting that these membranes had highly efficient anti-bacterial functionality (Fig. 5).

Based on the amine groups with lone electron pairs anchored on the Am-CD surfaces, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) pathway was likely inhibited by protons, and the original fluorescence enhancement and dual-platform-like fluorescence, as well as UV–vis absorption, occurred (Fig. 2C and D) [53]. When the Am-CD aqueous solution was reversibly titrated with 1.0 M of NaOH and HCl, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra almost completely overlapped (Fig. S9). The results reveal that the protonation of Am-CDs did not generate new irreversible species, and only the combination of protons with a lone electron pair of amines formed a -C-NH3 + group that could reduce the water contact angle of the membranes. With an increase in the pH of the Am-CDs aqueous solution, the -C-NH3 + group of the Am-CDs lost a proton and was reduced as the original -C-NH2 group. No significant differences in the XPS spectra between the pristine Am-CDs and Am-CDs treated with HCl were found (data not shown), also indicating the formation of reversible species. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of the reversibly titrating I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M immersed in water provided the same results (Fig. 7C and D).

The anti-chlorine mechanism of the NF membranes was subsequently considered. FTIR and XPS spectra were obtained to characterize the chlorinated Am-CDs. The peaks at 645 and 1390 cm−1, attributed to nitryl and C–Cl, respectively, were observed in the FTIR curve of the oxidized Am-CDs (Fig. 8 A). Compared to the XPS survey curve of the Am-CDs, a chlorine element was newly found in the XPS survey curve of the Am-CDs treated with NaClO (Fig. S10). The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum reveals three peaks with corresponding binding energies of ∼398.8, ∼399.5, and ∼403.9 eV, which could be assigned to –NH2, –NO2, and C–N, respectively (Fig. 8B) [56], indicating that some of the amines in the Am-CDs were oxidized as nitryl groups. A new type of C–Cl species with a binding energy of ∼198.7 eV, as well as Cl2p2/3 with a binding energy of 199.1 eV, and Cl2p1/2 with a binding energy of 200.45 eV, were discovered in the high-resolution Cl2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 8C) [56,62], indicating that the chlorination reaction took place. The hydrophobic C–Cl and C–NO2 moieties responded to an increase in the water contact angle of the chlorinated membranes. Notably, after the addition of AA to the oxidized Am-CDs aqueous solution, the nitryl group could be partially reduced to an amine group, resulting in partial recovery of quenched fluorescence (Fig. 8D) [56,62]. Therefore, the dual integrations of Am-CDs contributed to the excellent anti-acid/-chlorine performance of I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mg2+/Li+ separation performance of the nanofiltration membrane
ce:section.ce:para: II-Am-CDs-M was applied for Mg2+/Li+ separation, and their separation performance was evaluated (Scheme 1F). As illustrated in Fig. 9 A, for both MgCl2 and LiCl as feed solutions, the water flux and salt rejection of II-Am-CDs-M gradually decreased with increasing salt concentration from 1000 to 5000 ppm. At any given concentration, the water fluxes resulting from the use of LiCl as a feed solution were greater than those resulting from the use of MgCl2 as a feed solution; however, the opposite trend was observed for salt rejection. These results could be attributed to the following factors. The high osmotic pressure in reverse partially counteracted the pressure applied, decreasing the driving force for mass transport [64]. The counter ions were endlessly adsorbed on the positively charged surface of II-Am-CDs-M during the membrane process, impairing the Donnan effect [65]. A decrease in both water flux and salt rejection was subsequently observed at high salt concentrations. A series of mixed feed solutions (total concentration 2000 ppm) with different Li+/Mg2+ ratios were tested at 3 bar to evaluate Li+/Mg2+ selectivity. With a change in Li+/Mg2+ ratio from 1:1 to 1:100, the water flux remained almost constant; however, when the Li+/Mg2+ ratio in the feed was 1:50, the Contrast-M Li+/Mg2+ selectivity was only 6.1 (Fig. S12), the I-Am-CDs-M Li+/Mg2+ selectivity was 18.5 (Fig. S13), and the II-Am-CDs-M Li+/Mg2+ selectivity gradually increased to 21.4 and then decreased again (Fig. 9B). It may be due to the charge shielding effect caused by the high salt concentration, which can weaken the electrostatic exclusion (Donnan effect) of the membrane [32]. As a result, II-Am-CDs-M could be used as a filter to efficiently increase the Li+/Mg2+ ratio in the feed mixture.

The mechanism of Mg2+/Li+ separation was simply elucidated. The high salt rejection for large cations exhibited by the low positive charge Contrast-M indicated that the size sieving effect contributed to the separation of ions (Fig. 4B). However, the highly positively charged II-Am-CDs-M separated cations with high valence, revealing the presence of the Donnan effect (Fig. 9C). The synergy of the size sieving and the Donnan effect achieved ion separation in high salt mixtures, and the latter was dominant. Compared to previous reports (Table S2), the II-Am-CDs-M reported in this study had a greater isoelectric point (pH of 8.7), as well as relatively prominent separation performance, which was more suitable for separation applications over wider pH ranges. Moreover, the low-cost and easy-to-prepare Am-CDs with dual coupling onto the NF membrane endowed the membrane with anti-bacterial/-acid/-chlorine performance, benefiting the commercial operation of the NF membrane.

In summary, we fabricated Am-CDs using CA and PEI as precursors using a hydrothermal method and Am-CDs/PL nanocomposites to couple Am-CDs on PL via EDC/NHS catalysis. I-Am-CDs-M and II-Am-CDs-M were also prepared through the conjugation of Am-CDs by IP and SIP, respectively. The integration of Am-CDs endowed these NF membranes with improved hydrophilicity and more positive charges than Contrast-M, which gave rise to significantly higher water flux and salt rejection. Importantly, due to the good enzymatic activity and pH/ClO−-response performance of the Am-CDs, the modified NF membranes naturally inherited outstanding biofouling/acid/chlorine resistant properties. Additional investigations revealed that the anti-acid and anti-chlorine performance of the membranes resulted in the formation of reversible species (C–NH2–C–NH3 +) and semi-reversible species (C–NH2–C–NO2, oxidant: NaClO, reductant: AA. Note, only some of C–NO2 were reduced to C–NH2), respectively, which was conducive to the highly efficient operation of the membranes. II-Am-CDs-M was successfully applied for Mg2+/Li+ separation, reaching a higher Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of 21.4. Exploitation of the novel NF membranes provided a new horizon for decreasing maintenance costs and extending the operational lifespan.
